topic: Someone learns their favorite author plagiarized their stories
raw_output:
[story 1]

Elara traced the familiar lines of the first chapter with a fingertip, a strange, cold knot tightening in her chest. Every carefully crafted sentence, every nuanced character arc she had poured her soul into, seemed to have been mirrored, but twisted, in the work of Silas Vance. She reread the passage, her breath catching in her throat. It wasn't just similarity; it was a chilling, almost surgical replication of her unique narrative voice. A slow, creeping sense of disorientation washed over her. She pushed the manuscript away, the paper suddenly feeling flimsy and untrue beneath her touch. "This can't be right," she murmured to the empty study, her voice thin and reedy. She stood up abruptly, pacing the length of the room, her hands gripping the edge of the mahogany desk until her knuckles were white. The air in the room felt thick, pressurized.

[story 2]

Mark stared at the screen, the cursor blinking mockingly on the page. He had spent three years building the world of Aethelgard, brick by painstaking brick. Now, seeing the prose of Vance—so uncannily familiar, so deeply resonant with his own internal landscape—a hollow feeling settled deep in his stomach. He leaned back in his chair, the leather groaning beneath him, and slowly ran a hand through his already disheveled hair. His jaw tightened. He picked up his coffee mug, the ceramic warm against his palm, but the liquid offered no comfort. He took a long, deliberate sip, forcing his eyes back to the text. A tremor ran through his hand, causing the mug to wobble slightly. He set it down with a soft clink, the sound echoing unnervingly loud in the quiet apartment.

[story 3]

When Clara found the comparison, the silence in the library was absolute. She held the two texts side-by-side, the stark contrast between her original manuscript and Vance’s offering screaming in her mind. Her fingers began to shake uncontrollably, a rapid, shallow rhythm. She dropped the papers onto the antique table; the sound was sharp and final. Clara didn't move to gather them. Instead, she sank into the nearest armchair, pulling her knees up to her chest, burying her face in her arms. A strange pressure built behind her eyes, hot and insistent. She squeezed her eyes shut, trying to filter the overwhelming sense of trespass, the feeling that someone had stolen the very blueprint of her imagination and rebranded it as their own.
